sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 8, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: RIGHT movement caused the sky blue element on the right end of the top bar to swap with the blue element - the sky blue cursor moved from cols 41-42 to cols 39-40 (leftward by 2). The left-side blue/sky blue box didn't change. The cursor seems to move along the bar.
- Plan: Test more RIGHT presses to see the full range, then try LEFT to reverse. Need to understand the cursor mechanics and goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 7 of about 36 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The cursor only moved once (from cols 41-42 to 39-40) and then stopped. Additional RIGHT presses didn't move it further. So the cursor can only move one step in each direction. Let me try LEFT to see if it moves back, and also try UP/DOWN to see what they do.
I need to also consider: maybe the cursor is the sky blue region on the LEFT side (node 3, inside node 2), not the right side. The right side swap might be something else. Let me think about this differently.
Looking at the initial state:
- Left: Blue (b) 6x6 box with sky blue (S) 4x4 inside - this looks like a "player" or "cursor" container
- Middle: Gray (g) horizontal bar
- Right: Blue (b) 4x2 + sky blue (S) 4x2 - this looks like two adjacent blocks
After pressing RIGHT once:
- The right-side blue and sky blue swapped positions
- The left-side box didn't change
After pressing RIGHT 4 more times: nothing changed
So RIGHT seems to toggle/swap the two right-end blocks once. Let me try LEFT to see if it reverses this.
Actually, I'm wasting actions. Let me think about what the goal might be. The right panel has a 3x3 grid with a checkerboard pattern. The left panel has purple markers. Maybe the goal is to match the pattern from the left panel onto the right panel?
Let me look at the left panel pattern:
- Purple 2x2 blocks at positions that form a cross/plus shape within the 8x8 interior
- Top: (51-52, 15-16) - center top
- Left: (54-55, 12-13) - middle left
- Right: (54-55, 18-19) - middle right  
- Bottom: (57-58, 15-16) - center bottom
This forms a cross/plus pattern.
The right panel has:
Row 0: gray, white, gray
Row 1: white, gray, white
Row 2: gray, white, gray
This is a checkerboard pattern. The center is gray, and the 4 corners are gray, while the 4 edges are white.
Hmm, maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on specific blocks to change them? Let me try clicking on the gray blocks to turn them white (or vice versa) to match the cross pattern from the left panel.
But first, let me try LEFT to reverse the RIGHT effect, and then try MOUSE on the grid blocks again.
Wait, I should first try UP and DOWN to see if they do something different. Maybe the movement controls affect different parts of the interface.
Actually, let me be more strategic. Let me try LEFT first to reverse, then try UP/DOWN, and also try clicking on the left panel (purple markers) to see if that does something.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT moved the sky blue cursor on the right end one step left (cols 41-42 → 39-40). Further RIGHT presses had no effect - it seems to be a single-step toggle. Need to test LEFT to reverse and UP/DOWN for other effects. The goal likely involves matching patterns between the left panel (purple cross pattern) and the right panel (checkerboard grid).
Plan: Test LEFT to reverse the cursor, then test UP/DOWN. Also try MOUSE on the left panel purple markers.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# First try LEFT to see if it reverses the RIGHT effect
print("=== Testing LEFT ===")
action(['LEFT'])
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['b', 'S'] and n['pixels'] <= 16:
        b = n['boundary']
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Now test UP
print("\n=== Testing UP ===")
action(['UP'])
changed = last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)
print(f"  Board changed: {changed}")
if changed:
    for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
        if n['color'] in ['b', 'S'] and n['pixels'] <= 16:
            b = n['boundary']
            cols = [p[1] for p in b]
            rows = [p[0] for p in b]
            print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Now test DOWN
print("\n=== Testing DOWN ===")
action(['DOWN'])
changed = last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)
print(f"  Board changed: {changed}")
if changed:
    for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
        if n['color'] in ['b', 'S'] and n['pixels'] <= 16:
            b = n['boundary']
            cols = [p[1] for p in b]
            rows = [p[0] for p in b]
            print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Try MOUSE on a purple marker in the left panel
print("\n=== Testing MOUSE on purple marker (row 51, col 15) ===")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 51, 'col': 15}])
changed = last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)
print(f"  Board changed: {changed}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
